serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one slice of white toast (approximately 35 g) spread with a standard portion of butter, as commonly consumed at breakfast.

nutrient_source.title: Toast with butter — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05020114 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are preserved from this canonical source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: White bread GI is well-established at approximately 70–75 (mean ~73) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Toast with butter is treated as white bread toasted; butter addition does not materially lower GI. GI of 73 used as the best defensible estimate. GL per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 73 × (44.89 × 0.035) / 100 = 73 × 15.71 / 100 ≈ 11.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 443 mg/100 g combined with saturated fat ~8.2 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit toast with butter due to its borderline-high sodium content (443 mg/100 g) and saturated fat load from butter (~8.2 g/100 g), which together contribute to elevated blood pressure and arterial stiffness; substituting unsalted butter or avocado spread and choosing wholemeal bread will reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~73) with 44.9 g/100 g carbohydrate and low dietary fibre (2.38 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should consume toast with butter cautiously because white toast carries a high GI (~73) and delivers 44.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 2.38 g dietary fibre, producing rapid postprandial glucose excursions; pairing with a protein source and switching to wholemeal or sourdough bread can meaningfully reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~8.2 g/100 g from butter and cholesterol 45.82 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, the saturated fat content (~8.2 g/100 g) from butter and the cholesterol load (45.82 mg/100 g) are clinically significant; replacing butter with extra-virgin olive oil or avocado and choosing high-fibre bread will reduce LDL-raising saturated fat and support better cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~8.2 g/100 g from butter exceeds the 5 g RA threshold; estimated trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit toast with butter because butter's saturated fat (~8.2 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g pro-inflammatory threshold and its small trans fat content (~0.1 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory at any amount; substituting extra-virgin olive oil or avocado spread and using wholemeal or sourdough bread will reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Toast with butter is a simple, widely consumed breakfast item consisting of white bread that has been toasted until golden-brown and spread with a generous portion of salted butter. The toasting process creates a crisp, lightly caramelised exterior through the Maillard reaction while the interior remains slightly soft. The butter melts into the warm surface, imparting a rich, creamy, and mildly salty flavour. Per 100 g, this food delivers 371 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 18.14 g fat (of which approximately 8.2 g is saturated fat from butter), and 44.89 g carbohydrate with only 2.38 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 443 mg/100 g. The high glycemic index (~73) and low fibre content make this food a high-glycaemic-load option that can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose rises, making it a concern for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. It is commonly served at breakfast across Western and Westernised contexts globally, including Singapore where it appears as a standard café and hawker breakfast item. Clinically, the combination of refined carbohydrate, saturated fat, and moderate sodium warrants caution for metabolic and cardiovascular conditions. Wholemeal or sourdough substitutions with unsalted butter or avocado spread are recommended for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020114, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). The source is an approved primary regional source. GI is estimated from the well-established white bread GI value (~73) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the closest defensible approved GI source for this food.
notes: saturated_fat_g of 8.2 g/100 g is estimated from the butter component based on standard butter composition (approximately 45% of total fat as saturated fat); this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source and is documented as an estimate. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g is estimated from the butter component (naturally occurring ruminant trans fat); not explicitly reported in HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite food. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail not available in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite food as of 2026. This is a Western/international generic food item; no Singaporean-specific multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) is required under the multilingual rules, as this food does not originate from or belong to a Singaporean cuisine category requiring mandatory local-language names.
